metadata.summary: Canonical patterns for orchestrating AI agents: Supervisor, Hierarchical, Sequential, Peer-to-Peer. Design checklists and production-readiness gates.

content.title: Multi-Agent Design Patterns
content.summary: Four canonical patterns for orchestrating multiple AI agents: Supervisor, Hierarchical, Sequential, and Peer-to-Peer. Choose the simplest pattern; includes design checklists and production-readiness gates.

why.p: Single agents fail on complex enterprise workflows because of context window limits, tool access isolation requirements, and latency constraints. Multi-agent systems apply the microservices principle: specialized agents collaborate through typed shared state, with each agent owning a narrow domain. Gartner recorded a 1,445% surge in multi-agent inquiries from Q1 2024 to Q2 2025.

list.item: Single agent context window is insufficient for the full task
Tasks have parallelizable subtasks (research + writing + validation can run concurrently)
Domain expertise must be isolated — a billing agent must not have access to CRM tools
Enterprise workflows map naturally to organizational units (teams, departments, roles)
Reliability requires cross-checking: parallel agents can validate each other's outputs
Simple single-step tasks — multi-agent adds coordination overhead with no benefit
Latency is critical (<2s) — agent-to-agent round trips add 500ms–2s each
The problem is not well-decomposed yet — build a working single agent first, then extract workers
Token budget is constrained — multi-agent systems use significantly more tokens per task
Infrastructure cannot reliably run concurrent agent processes

section.name: supervisor-pattern
section.p: The Supervisor pattern centralizes orchestration: a single coordinator agent routes requests to specialized worker agents based on task classification.

rule.statement: Use Supervisor when tasks map to distinct worker domains and centralized control is acceptable.
rule.rationale: A single coordinator routes requests to specialized workers via LLM reasoning or rule-based matching. Predictable, debuggable, but creates a single point of failure.

section.p: Supported by: LangGraph (StateGraph + supervisor node), CrewAI (manager agent), AutoGen (GroupChat), Google ADK (transfer_to_agent).

list.item: Good for: multi-domain help desks, intelligent routing, centralized monitoring
Risk: supervisor bottleneck; LLM routing is probabilistic — ambiguous inputs can route incorrectly

section.name: hierarchical-pattern
section.p: The Hierarchical pattern applies recursive decomposition across organizational units. Higher-level agents break down goals into sub-tasks for lower-level agents.

rule.statement: Use Hierarchical when the problem requires recursive decomposition across organizational units; limit to 2–3 levels to avoid coordination overhead.
rule.rationale: Multi-level tree where higher agents decompose goals into sub-tasks for lower agents. Scales well but each coordination layer adds 500ms+ of LLM call overhead.

list.item: Good for: complex enterprise workflows with cross-functional teams, large report generation
Risk: "middle management" latency; difficult to debug across levels without distributed tracing

section.name: sequential-pattern
section.p: The Sequential pattern implements linear workflows where each stage depends on the previous one. Agents are invoked in order, with each stage writing typed output for the next stage.

rule.statement: Use Sequential for linear workflows where stage N depends on stage N-1; use output_key per stage to pass typed state.
rule.rationale: Assembly-line pipeline: each agent reads the previous stage output from shared state and writes its result under a named key. Deterministic, easy to debug, but no fault isolation — failure at any stage blocks all downstream stages.

section.p: Supported by: Google ADK (SequentialAgent), LangGraph (linear graph edges), CrewAI (sequential process).

list.item: Good for: ETL pipelines, document transformation chains (parse → extract → summarize → format)
Risk: no parallelism; single failure stops the pipeline

section.name: peer-to-peer-pattern
section.p: The Peer-to-Peer pattern enables decentralized architectures where agents communicate directly without a central orchestrator. Each agent routes to peers based on its local routing rules.

rule.statement: Use Peer-to-Peer only when resilience and fault tolerance outweigh debugging complexity; enforce DAG structure to prevent circular routing.
rule.rationale: Decentralized architecture where agents communicate directly without a central orchestrator. High fault tolerance but requires distributed tracing to understand message flow.

list.item: Good for: cross-organizational collaboration, privacy-preserving systems, adaptive agent networks
Risk: circular dependency potential; global state is hard to reason about

section.name: pattern-comparison
section.p: Each pattern trades off different qualities. No single pattern is universally best; selection depends on your specific constraints and priorities:

list.item: Control: Supervisor=centralized, Hierarchical=multi-level, Sequential=linear, P2P=distributed
Fault tolerance: Supervisor=low, Hierarchical=medium, Sequential=low, P2P=high
Debugging: Sequential=easiest, P2P=hardest

section.name: additional-patterns
section.p: Beyond the core four patterns, several derived patterns address specific use cases:

list.item: Parallel Fan-Out/Gather — spawn concurrent agents, synthesize results
Generator-Critic — one agent creates, another validates; run at least 2 critique passes
Loop (Iterative Refinement) — repeated improvement until criteria met; always set max_iterations
Human-in-the-Loop — explicit approval checkpoints in graph; must be designed in, not retrofitted
Router — dynamic tool/agent selection based on input classification

antipattern.description: Infinite loop: supervisor routes to agent A, which routes back to supervisor, which routes to A again.
antipattern.reason: Without a max_steps guard the loop never terminates. Set MAX_ITER and return a sentinel message to the user when reached.

antipattern.description: Token explosion: each agent forwards its full conversation history to the next agent.
antipattern.reason: History accumulates multiplicatively across multi-agent calls. Summarize or trim conversation history between agent steps.

antipattern.description: Parallel fan-out agents writing to shared state simultaneously.
antipattern.reason: Race conditions corrupt state. Use merge nodes with explicit conflict resolution.

section.name: pre-implementation-all-patterns
section.p: Before implementing any multi-agent system, establish clear boundaries and infrastructure:

rule.statement: Define clear agent role boundaries and tool access permissions before writing any prompts or code.
rule.rationale: Tool access leakage in frameworks like AutoGen lets agents see tool results intended for others; scoping prevents this.

list.item: Define clear problem scope and agent responsibilities
Choose communication mechanism: typed shared state (Pydantic models), message passing, or tool results
Select framework: LangGraph (state machines), CrewAI (role-based), Google ADK (native primitives), AutoGen (conversations)
Plan error handling and fallback strategies per agent
Design logging and distributed tracing from day one
Establish max_iterations guard before writing the first agent loop
Design: list all task types, group into non-overlapping worker domains
Design: choose routing method — LLM-driven or rule-based; plan fallback for unclassified requests
Implement: supervisor with timeout handling and result aggregation
Implement: workers with isolated tool access and structured output
Test: routing accuracy, each worker in isolation, error fallbacks, concurrent load on supervisor
Production: add circuit breakers for failing workers; set alerting on supervisor failure
Design: define output_key for each stage; verify stage N output matches stage N+1 input schema
Design: plan state storage and cleanup after pipeline completion
Implement: checkpoint persistence between critical stages for recovery
Implement: early termination conditions when validation fails
Test: each stage independently; stage-to-stage data passing; pipeline recovery from checkpoint
Production: add per-stage retries for idempotent stages; measure stage-by-stage latency
Design: map organizational structure; define 2–3 levels maximum
Design: specify task specification format (parent to child) and result format (child to parent)
Implement: timeout cascading across levels
Test: each level in isolation; escalation scenarios; partial failure handling
Production: monitor each level independently; set up distributed tracing
Design: define routing strategy per agent; enforce DAG — no cycles
Design: plan authentication between agents and data sharing boundaries
Implement: message acknowledgment and dead-letter handling
Test: multi-hop routing; network partition scenarios; circular dependency prevention
Production: implement distributed tracing; monitor communication latency; plan agent version compatibility

rule.statement: Block deployment if any of these gates fails: role boundaries undefined, no distributed tracing, no max_iterations guard, no fallback path for primary agent failure.
rule.rationale: Multi-agent failures without traces are nearly impossible to debug; loops without guards drain budgets silently.

list.item: All agent roles clearly defined with scoped tool access
End-to-end latency measured and acceptable
All agents emit logs; distributed tracing works
Agent-to-agent auth implemented; prompt injection mitigations in place

template.purpose: Pydantic state models for all four patterns
YAML configuration for agent roles and routing rules
